Annual Exam - 2019

Code: 65/4/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 29 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:100

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper contains 29 questions divided into four sections A, B, C and D. Section A comprises of 4 questions of one mark each, Section B comprises of 8 questions of two marks each, Section C comprises of 11 questions of four marks each and Section D comprises of 6 questions of six marks each.
  3. All questions in Section A are to be answered in one word, one sentence or as per the exact requirement of the question.
  4. There is no overall choice. However, internal choice has been provided in 1 question of Section A, 3 questions of Section B, 3 questions of Section C and 3 questions of Section D. You have to attempt only one of the alternatives in all such questions.
  5. Use of calculators is not permitted. You may ask for logarithmic tables, if required.

SECTION A

Question numbers 1 to 4 carry 1 mark each.

1.

Form the differential equation representing the family of curves {tex}y=\frac{A}{x}+5{/tex}, by eliminating the arbitrary constant A. 
2.

If A is a square matrix of order 3, with |A| = 9, then write the value of |2.adjA|.
3.

Find the acute angle between the planes {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})=1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=0{/tex}.

OR

Find the length of the intercept, cut off by the plane 2x + y - z = 5 on the x-axis.
4.

If  y = log (cos ex), then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.

SECTION B

Question numbers 5 to 12 carry 2 marks each.

5.

Find:{tex}\int \limits_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{0} \frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x} d x{/tex}
6.

Let * be an operation defined as * : R {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R such that a * b = 2a + b, a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R. Check if * is a binary operation. If yes, find if it is associative too.
7.

X and Y are two points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-3 \vec{b}{/tex} respectively. Write the position vector of a point Z which divides the line segment XY in the ratio 2 : 1 externally.

OR

Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}  be two vectors. Show that the vectors {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} are perpendicular to each other.
8.

If A and B are symmetric matrices, such that AB and BA are both defined, then prove that AB – BA is a skew symmetric matrix.
9.

12 cards numbered 1 to 12 (one number on one card), are placed in a box and mixed up thoroughly. Then a card is drawn at random from the box. If it is known that the number on the drawn card is greater than 5, find the probability that the card bears an odd number.
10.

Out of 8 outstanding students of a school, in which there are 3 boys and  5 girls, a team of 4 students is to be selected for a quiz competition. Find the probability that 2 boys and 2 girls are selected.

OR

In a multiple-choice examination with three possible answers for each of the five questions, what is the probability that a candidate would get four or more correct answers just by guessing?
11.

Solve the following differential equation.
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}
12.

Find: {tex}\int x \cdot \tan ^{-1} x \ d x{/tex}

OR

Find: {tex}\int \frac { d x } { \sqrt { 5 - 4 x - 2 x ^ { 2 } } }.{/tex}

SECTION C

Question numbers 13 to 23 carry 4 marks each.

13.

Using properties of determinants, find the value of x for which {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 4-x & 4+x & 4+x \\ 4+x & 4-x & 4+x \\ 4+x & 4+x & 4-x \end{array}\right|=0{/tex}.
14.

Solve the differential equation {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 1 + x2 + y2 + x2y2, given that y = 1 when x = 0.

OR

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } {/tex}, given that y = 1 when x = 0.
15.

Let A = R - {2} and B = R - {1}. If f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is a function defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{x-1}{x-2}{/tex}, show that f is one-one and onto. Hence, find f-1.

OR

Show that the relation R in the set A = {tex}\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{Z}: \mathbf{0} \leq \mathbf{x} \leq 12\}{/tex} given by R =  {(a, b) : a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z, {tex}|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|{/tex} is divisible by 3} is an equivalence relation.
16.

Integrate the function {tex}\frac{\cos (x+a)}{\sin (x+b)}{/tex} w.r.t.x.
17.

If x = sin t, y = sin pt, prove that {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+p^2 y=0{/tex}.

OR

Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} with respect to cos-1x2.
18.

Prove that {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 12 } { 13 } \right) + \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right) = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 56 } { 65 } \right).{/tex}
19.

If y = (x)cos x + (cos x)sin x, find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
20.

Prove that {tex}\int\limits_0^a f(x) d x=\int\limits_0^a f(a-x) d x{/tex}, and hence evaluate {tex}\int\limits_0^1 x^2(1-x)^n d x{/tex}.
21.

Find the value of x, for which the four points A(x, -1, -1), B(4, 5, 1), C(3, 9, 4) and D(- 4, 4, 4) are coplanar.
22.

A ladder 13 m long is leaning against a vertical wall. The bottom of the ladder is dragged away from the wall along the ground at the rate of 2 cm/sec. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 5 m away from the wall?
23.

Find the vector equation of the plane determined by the points A(3, -1, 2), B(5, 2, 4) and C(-1, -1, 6). Hence, find the distance of the plane, thus obtained, from the origin.

SECTION D

Question numbers 24 to 29 carry 6 marks each.

24.

Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex}.
25.

An insurance company insured 3000 cyclists, 6000 scooter drivers and 9000 car drivers. The probability of an accident involving a cyclist, a scooter driver and a car driver are 0·3, 0·05 and 0·02 respectively. One of the insured persons meets with an accident. What is the probability that he is a cyclist?
26.

Using elementary row transformations, find the inverse of the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

OR

Using matrices, solve the following system of linear equations:
x + 2y - 3z = -4
2x + 3y + 2z = 2
3x - 3y - 4z = 11
27.

Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the parabola y2 = 4x and the circle 4x2 + 4y2 = 9.

OR

Using the method of integration, find the area of the region bounded by the lines 3x - 2y + 1 = 0, 2x + 3y - 21 = 0 and x - 5y + 9 = 0.
28.

A dietician wishes to mix two types of food in such a way that the vitamin contents of the mixture contains at least 8 units of vitamin A and 10 units of vitamin C. Food I contains 2 units/kg of vitamin A and 1 unit/kg of vitamin C. It costs ₹ 50 per kg to produce food I. Food II contains 1 unit/kg of vitamin A and 2 units/kg of vitamin C and it costs ₹ 70 per kg to produce food II. Formulate this problem as a LPP to minimise the cost of a mixture that will produce the required diet. Also find the minimum cost.
29.

Find the vector equation of a line passing through the point (2, 3, 2) and parallel to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}. Also, find the distance between these two lines.

OR

Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular Q drawn from  P(3, 2, 1) to the plane 2x – y + z + 1 = 0. Also, find the distance PQ and the image of the point P treating this plane as a mirror.

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2019

Code: 65/4/1 (set-1)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 29 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:100

General Instructions :

  1. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper contains 29 questions divided into four sections A, B, C and D. Section A comprises of 4 questions of one mark each, Section B comprises of 8 questions of two marks each, Section C comprises of 11 questions of four marks each and Section D comprises of 6 questions of six marks each.
  3. All questions in Section A are to be answered in one word, one sentence or as per the exact requirement of the question.
  4. There is no overall choice. However, internal choice has been provided in 1 question of Section A, 3 questions of Section B, 3 questions of Section C and 3 questions of Section D. You have to attempt only one of the alternatives in all such questions.
  5. Use of calculators is not permitted. You may ask for logarithmic tables, if required.

SECTION A

Question numbers 1 to 4 carry 1 mark each.

1.

Form the differential equation representing the family of curves {tex}y=\frac{A}{x}+5{/tex}, by eliminating the arbitrary constant A. 

Solution:

The given differential equation is,

{tex}y=\frac{A}{x}+5{/tex}

xy = A + 5x

Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get,

 {tex} x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=5{/tex}

 1
2.

If A is a square matrix of order 3, with |A| = 9, then write the value of |2.adjA|.

Solution:

we have ,|2.adj A| = 23 |A|3−1 = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 81 = 648

 1
3.

Find the acute angle between the planes {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-2 \hat{k})=1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(3 \hat{i}-6 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k})=0{/tex}.

Solution:

Let {tex}\theta {/tex} be an angle between vectors.

So,

{tex}\cos \theta=\frac{3+12-4}{3 \times 7}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{11}{21}\right){/tex}

 1

OR

Find the length of the intercept, cut off by the plane 2x + y - z = 5 on the x-axis.

Solution:

Equation of the plane is  {tex}2x + y - z = 5{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{2x}}{5} + \frac{y}{5} - \frac{z}{5} = 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x}{{\left( {\frac{5}{2}} \right)}} + \frac{y}{5} +\frac{z}{-5} = 1{/tex}
Comparing with intercept form {tex}\frac{x}{a} + \frac{y}{b} + \frac{z}{c} = 1{/tex}, we have {tex}a = \frac{5}{2},b = 5,c = - 5{/tex} which are intercepts cut off by the plane on x - axis, y - axis and z - axis respectively.

 1
4.

If  y = log (cos ex), then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\sin e^{x}}{\cos e^{x}} \cdot e^{x}{/tex} [using chain rule]
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -ex . tan ex

 1

SECTION B

Question numbers 5 to 12 carry 2 marks each.

5.

Find:{tex}\int \limits_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{0} \frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x} d x{/tex}

Solution:

={tex}\int \limits_{-\frac{\pi}{4}}^{0} \frac{tan(\frac{\pi}{4})+\tan x}{1-tan(\frac{\pi}{4})\tan x} d x{/tex}

={tex}\int \limits_{-\pi / 4}^{0} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right) dx{/tex} = {tex}\log \left|\sec \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)\right|_{-\pi / 4}^{0}{/tex}
= {tex}\log \sqrt{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{2} \log 2{/tex}

 2
6.

Let * be an operation defined as * : R {tex}\times{/tex} R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R such that a * b = 2a + b, a, b {tex}\in{/tex} R. Check if * is a binary operation. If yes, find if it is associative too.

Solution:

{tex}{a}^{*} {b}=2 {a}+{b} \in {R} \quad \forall {a}, {b} \in {R}{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore{/tex} * is binary
a * (b * c) = a * (2b + c) = 2a + 2b + c
(a * b) * c = (2a + b) * c = 4a + 2b + c
In general a * (b* c) {tex}\neq{/tex} (a * b) * c
{tex}\therefore{/tex} * is not associative

 2
7.

X and Y are two points with position vectors {tex}3 \vec{a}+\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{a}-3 \vec{b}{/tex} respectively. Write the position vector of a point Z which divides the line segment XY in the ratio 2 : 1 externally.

Solution:

Position vector of z = {tex}\frac{2(\vec{a}-3 \vec{b})-1(3 \vec{a}+\vec{b})}{2-1}{/tex}
= {tex}-\vec{a}-7 \vec{b}{/tex},
This is the required solution.

 2

OR

Let {tex}\vec{a}=\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}  be two vectors. Show that the vectors {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}){/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} are perpendicular to each other.

Solution:

We have,{tex}\vec{a}+\vec{b}=4 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}-5 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 8 + 5 + 3 = 0
Therefore {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \perp(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex}

 2
8.

If A and B are symmetric matrices, such that AB and BA are both defined, then prove that AB – BA is a skew symmetric matrix.

Solution:

We are Given: A' = A, B' = B
(AB - BA)' = (AB)' - (BA)'
= B'A' - A'B'
= BA - AB
= - (AB - BA),
Hence AB - BA is skew symmetric.

 2
9.

12 cards numbered 1 to 12 (one number on one card), are placed in a box and mixed up thoroughly. Then a card is drawn at random from the box. If it is known that the number on the drawn card is greater than 5, find the probability that the card bears an odd number.

Solution:

Consider the following events:

A: card bears odd number
B: Number on the cards is greater than 5
{tex}A \cap B{/tex} = {7, 9, 11}
{tex}P(A | B){/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 / 12}{7 /1 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}

 2
10.

Out of 8 outstanding students of a school, in which there are 3 boys and  5 girls, a team of 4 students is to be selected for a quiz competition. Find the probability that 2 boys and 2 girls are selected.

Solution:

Required probability is given by,

 {tex}\frac{^{3} \mathrm{C}_{2} \times^{5} \mathrm{C}_{2}}{^{8} \mathrm{C}_{4}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{3×10}{70}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{3}{7}{/tex}

 2

OR

In a multiple-choice examination with three possible answers for each of the five questions, what is the probability that a candidate would get four or more correct answers just by guessing?

Solution:

{tex}p = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} and q = 1 - p {tex} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}
n = 5, r = 4, 5 and P (X = r) = {tex}^n{C_r}{p^r}{q^{n - r}}{/tex}
P (Four or more success) = P (X = 4) + P (X = 5)

Solve the following differential equation.
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}

Solution:

Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + y = \cos x - \sin x{/tex}
which is a linear differential equation of the form
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } + P y = Q{/tex}
Here, P = 1 and Q = cos x - sinx
{tex}\therefore \quad \mathrm { IF } = e ^ { \int P d { x } } = e ^ { \int 1 d x } = e ^ { x }{/tex}
We  know that the solution of linear differential equation is given by
{tex}y \times \mathrm { IF } = \int ( Q \times \text { IF } ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad y e ^ { x } = \int e ^ { x } ( \cos x - \sin x ) d x + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y e ^ { x } = e ^ { x } \cos x + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \int e ^ { x } \left( f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C\right.{/tex} where {tex}f ( x ) = \cos x ; f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = - \sin x ]{/tex}
Therefore,on dividing both sides by ex, we get,
y = cos x + Ce-x
which is the required solution.

Find the particular solution of the differential equation {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } {/tex}, given that y = 1 when x = 0.

Solution:

Given differential equation is
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x y } { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \frac { y } { x } } { 1 + \frac { y ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}  .....(i)
Which is a homogeneous differential equation as {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = F \left( \frac { y } { x } \right){/tex}.
Now, put y = vx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = v + x \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex},then  from Eq.(i), we get
{tex}v + x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } - v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { v - v - v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac { d v } { d x } = - \frac { v ^ { 3 } } { 1 + v ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 + v ^ { 2 } } { v ^ { 3 } } d v = - \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
On integrating both sides, we get
{tex}\int \left( \frac { 1 } { v ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 1 } { v } \right) d v = - \int \frac { d x } { x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { 1 } { 2 v ^ { 2 } } + \log | v | = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log \left| \frac { y } { x } \right| = - \log | x | + C \left[ \text { put } v = \frac { y } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | - \log | x | = - \log | x | + C{/tex}
{tex}\left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = C{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, it is given that y = 1, when x = 0.
From Eq. (ii), we have
0 + log|1| = C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 0
On putting C = 0 in Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}- \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } + \log | y | = 0 \Rightarrow \log | y | = \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y = e ^ { \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 y ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
which is the required solution.

Let A = R - {2} and B = R - {1}. If f : A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} B is a function defined by f(x) = {tex}\frac{x-1}{x-2}{/tex}, show that f is one-one and onto. Hence, find f-1.

Solution:

Given f(x) = {tex}\frac{x-1}{x-2}{/tex}
To show f is one-one:
Let f(x1) = f(x2)
then {tex}\frac{x_1 -1}{x_1 -2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x_2 -1}{x_2 -2}{/tex}
or (x1 - 1)(x2 - 2) = (x1 - 2)(x2 - 1)
or x1x2 - 2x1 - x2 + 2 = x1x2 - x1 - 2x2 + 2
or -2x1 - x2 = -x1 - 2x2
or -2x1 + x1 = x2 - 2x2
or -x1 = -x2
or x1 = x2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is one-one.
To show f is onto:
Let y {tex}\in{/tex} B
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = f(x)
or y = {tex}\frac{x-1}{x-2}{/tex}
or y(x - 2) = x -1 
or xy - 2y = x - 1
or xy - x = 2y - 1
or x(y - 1) = 2y - 1
or x = {tex}\frac{2y-1}{y-1}{/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} B = R = -{1}
For every value of y except y = 1, there is a pre-image x = {tex}\frac{2y-1}{y-1}{/tex} = g(y), g(y) {tex}\ne{/tex} 2.
or x {tex}\in{/tex} A
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is onto
Since, f is one-one and onto, therefore f is invertible
Thus f-1(x) = {tex}\frac{2x-1}{x-1}{/tex} = g(x)

Show that the relation R in the set A = {tex}\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbf{Z}: \mathbf{0} \leq \mathbf{x} \leq 12\}{/tex} given by R =  {(a, b) : a, b {tex}\in{/tex} Z, {tex}|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|{/tex} is divisible by 3} is an equivalence relation.

Solution:

For reflexive:
Clearly, (a,a) {tex}\in{/tex} R, as |a - a| = 0 is always divisible by 3.
For Symmetry:
Let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |a - b| is divisible by 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} |b - a| is divisible by 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (b, a) {tex}\in{/tex} R
Therefore, R is Symmetric.
For Transitivity:
Let (a, b) {tex}\in{/tex} R & (b, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|a-b|{/tex} = 3m, and {tex}|b-c|{/tex} =  3n
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - b = {tex}\pm 3 \mathrm{m}{/tex}
and b - c = {tex}\pm 3 \mathrm{n}{/tex}
Therefore, a - c = {tex}3(\pm \mathrm{m} \pm \mathrm{n}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a - c is divisible by 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{c}|{/tex} is divisible by 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (a, c) {tex}\in{/tex} R
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R is reflexive, symmetric and transitive
{tex}\therefore{/tex} R is an equivalence reaction.

A ladder 13 m long is leaning against a vertical wall. The bottom of the ladder is dragged away from the wall along the ground at the rate of 2 cm/sec. How fast is the height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 5 m away from the wall?

Solution:

Let y m be the height of the wall at which the ladder touches. Also, let the foot of the ladder be x m away from the wall.
Then, by Pythagoras theorem, we have:

{tex} x^2+y^2=169 {/tex} .. [length of the ladder is 13 m]
{tex} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{169-x^2} {/tex}

Then, the rate of change of height {tex}(y){/tex} with respect to time {tex}(t){/tex} is given by,

{tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{-{x}}{\sqrt{169-{x}^2}} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

Now, when {tex}x=5 {~m}{/tex}, we have:

{tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{-2 \times 5}{\sqrt{169-5^2}}=\frac{-10}{\sqrt{144}}=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex}

Hence, the height of the ladder on the wall is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{5}{6} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex}.

{tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} x-3 & y+1 & z-2 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ -4 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = 0
12(x - 3) - 16(y + 1) + 12(z - 2) = 0
3x - 4y + 3z = 19
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}})=19{/tex}

24.

Using integration, find the area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in an ellipse {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1{/tex}.

Solution:

Let ABCD be the rectangle of maximum area with sides AB = 2x and BC = 2y, where C(x, y) is a point on the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1{/tex} as shown in the Fig.

The area A of the rectangle is 4xy i.e. A = 4xy which gives {tex}{A^2} = 16{x^2}{y^2} = s{/tex} (say)
Therefore, {tex}s = 16{x^2}\left( {1 - \frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}}} \right).{b^2} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( {{a^2}{x^2} - {x^4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}.\left[ {2{a^2}x - 4{x^3}} \right].{/tex} 
Again, {tex}\frac{{ds}}{{dx}} = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} and {tex}y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\,\left[ {2{a^2} - 12{x^2}} \right]{/tex} 
At {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},\frac{{{d^2}s}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left[ {2{a^2} - 6{a^2}} \right] = \frac{{16{b^2}}}{{{a^2}}}\left( { - 4{a^2}} \right) < 0{/tex} 
Thus, at {tex}x = \frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }},y = \frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }},s{/tex} is maximum and hence the area A is maximum.
Maximum area  {tex} = 4.x.y = 4.\frac{a}{{\sqrt 2 }}.\frac{b}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 2{/tex} ab sq units.

25.

An insurance company insured 3000 cyclists, 6000 scooter drivers and 9000 car drivers. The probability of an accident involving a cyclist, a scooter driver and a car driver are 0·3, 0·05 and 0·02 respectively. One of the insured persons meets with an accident. What is the probability that he is a cyclist?

Solution:

E1 : Selected person is cyclist
E2 : Selected person is scooter driver
E3 : Selected person is car driver
A : insured person met with an accident

P(E1) = {tex}\frac{3}{18}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{6}{18}{/tex}, P(E3) = {tex}\frac{9}{18}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right){/tex} = 0.3, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.05, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right){/tex} = 0.02
{tex}P\left(E_{1} / A\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}}{\frac{3}{18} \times \frac{30}{100}+\frac{6}{18} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{9}{18} \times \frac{2}{100}}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{90}{138}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}

Required Probability = {tex}\frac{15}{23}{/tex}

Solution:

Let, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 

We can write it as, A = IA
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -3 & 5 \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A
{tex}R_{1} \rightarrow R_{1}-R_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -4 & 7 \\ 3 & 2 & -4 \\ 1 & 1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A

{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -4 & 7 \\ 0 & 14 & -25 \\ 0 & 5 & -9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ -3 & 1 & 3 \\ -1 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow-\left(\mathrm{R}_{2}-3 \mathrm{R}_{3}\right){/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -4 & 7 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 5 & -9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & -1 & 3 \\ -1 & 0 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+4 \mathrm{R}_{2}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{R}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{3}-5 \mathrm{R}_{2}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & -1 \\ 0 & 1 & -2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -4 & 11 \\ 0 & -1 & 3 \\ -1 & 5 & -13 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A
{tex}\mathrm{R}_{1} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+\mathrm{R}_{3}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{R}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}+2 \mathrm{R}_{3}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & -2 \\ -2 & 9 & -23 \\ -1 & 5 & -13 \end{array}\right]{/tex}A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & -2 \\ -2 & 9 & -23 \\ -1 & 5 & -13 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

27.

Using integration, find the area of the region bounded by the parabola y2 = 4x and the circle 4x2 + 4y2 = 9.

Solution:

x coordinate of Point of intersection = {tex}\frac {1} {2}{/tex}

Required Area = {tex}2\left[\int\limits_0^{\frac{1}{2}} 2 \sqrt{x} d x+\int\limits_{\frac{1}{2}}^{\frac{3}2} \sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2-x^2} d x\right]{/tex}

OR

Using the method of integration, find the area of the region bounded by the lines 3x - 2y + 1 = 0, 2x + 3y - 21 = 0 and x - 5y + 9 = 0.

Solution:

Given lines are 
3x - 2y + 1 ..(i)
2x + 3y - 21 = 0 ..(ii)
x- 5y + 9 = 0 ..(iii)
For intersection of (i) and (ii),
Multiply (i) by 3 and (ii) by 2, and adding we get
9x - 6y + 3 + 4x + 6y - 42 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13x - 39 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 
Putting it in (i), we get 
9 - 2y + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 5
Intersection point of (i) and (ii) is (3, 5)

For intersection of (ii) and (iii),
Multiply (iii) by 2 and applying (ii) - (iii) we get,
2x + 3y - 21 - 2x + 10y - 18 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13y - 39 = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3
Putting y = 3 in (ii), we get
2x + 9 - 21 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - 12 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6
Intersection point of (ii) and (iii) is (6, 3).
For intersection of (i) and (iii),
Multiply (iii) by 3 and applying (i) - (iii) we get
3x - 2y + 1 - 3x + 15y - 27 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 13y - 26 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2
Putting y = 2 in (i), we get
3x - 4 + 1 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
The intersection point of (i) and (iii) is (1, 2).
With the help of the point of intersection, we draw the graph of lines (i), (ii) and (iii). The shaded region is the required region.
Area of Required region
= {tex}\int_{1}^{3} \frac{3 x+1}{2} d x{/tex} + {tex}\int_{3}^{6} \frac{-2 x+21}{3} d x{/tex} - {tex}\int_{1}^{6} \frac{x+9}{5} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{2} \int_{1}^{3} x d x+\frac{1}{2} \int_{1}^{3} d x{/tex}- {tex}\frac{2}{3} \int_{3}^{6} x d x+7 \int_{3}^{6} d x{/tex}- {tex}\frac{1}{5} \int_{1}^{6} x d x-\frac{9}{5} \int_{1}^{6} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{2}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{3}+\frac{1}{2}[x]_{1}^{3}-\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{3}^{6}{/tex}+ {tex}7[x]_{3}^{6}-\frac{1}{5}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{1}^{6}-\frac{9}{5}[x]_{1}^{6}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{4}(9-1)+\frac{1}{2}(3-1)-\frac{2}{6}(36-9){/tex} + {tex}7(6-3)-\frac{1}{10}(36-1)-\frac{9}{5}(6-1){/tex}
= 6 + 1 - 9 + 21 - {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} - 9
= 10 - {tex}\frac{7}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{20-7}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{13}{2}{/tex}

28.

A dietician wishes to mix two types of food in such a way that the vitamin contents of the mixture contains at least 8 units of vitamin A and 10 units of vitamin C. Food I contains 2 units/kg of vitamin A and 1 unit/kg of vitamin C. It costs ₹ 50 per kg to produce food I. Food II contains 1 unit/kg of vitamin A and 2 units/kg of vitamin C and it costs ₹ 70 per kg to produce food II. Formulate this problem as a LPP to minimise the cost of a mixture that will produce the required diet. Also find the minimum cost.

Solution:

Let the mixture contain x kg of Food I and y kg of Food II. Clearly, {tex}x \geq 0{/tex}, {tex}y \geq 0{/tex}. We make the following table from the given data:

Resources Food Requirement
I (x) II (y)  
Vitamin A (units/kg) 2 1 8
Vitamin C (units/kg) 1 2 10
Cost (Rs/kg) 50 70  

Since the mixture must contain at least 8 units of vitamin A and 10 units of vitamin C, we have the constraints:
2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 8
x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 10
Total cost Z of purchasing x kg of food I and y kg of Food II is
Z = 50x + 70y
Hence, the mathematical formulation of the problem is:
Minimise Z = 50x + 70y ....(i)
subject to the constraints:
2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 8 ... (ii)
x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 10 ...(iii)
x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 .....(iv)
Let us graph the inequalities (ii) to (iv). The feasible region determined by the system is shown in Figure. Here again, observe that the feasible region is unbounded.
Let us evaluate Z at the corner points A(0,8), B(2,4) and C(10,0).

Corner point Z = 50x + 70y
(0, 8) 560
(2, 4) 380 Minimum
(10, 0) 500


In the table, we find that smallest value of Z is 380 at the point (2,4). Can we say that the minimum value of Z is 380 . Remember that the feasible region is unbounded. Therefore, we have to draw the graph of the inequality
50x + 70y < 380 i.e., 5x + 7y < 38 , to check whether the resulting open half plane has any point common with the feasible region. From the Figure, we see that it has no points in common.
Thus, the minimum value of Z is 380 attained at the point (2, 4). Hence, the optimal mixing strategy for the dietician would be to mix 2 kg of Food ‘I’ and 4 kg of Food ‘II’, and with this strategy, the minimum cost of the mixture will be Rs 380.

Find the vector equation of a line passing through the point (2, 3, 2) and parallel to the line {tex}\vec{r}=(-2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}. Also, find the distance between these two lines.

Solution:

Required equation of line is
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{r}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}+\mu(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_1=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}, \vec{\mathrm{a}}_2=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}, \vec{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{\mathrm{a}}_2-\vec{\mathrm{a}}_1=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{d}=\frac{\left|\left(\vec{\mathrm{a}}_2-\vec{\mathrm{a}}_1\right) \times \vec{\mathrm{b}}\right|}{|\vec{\mathrm{b}}|}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \times \vec{b}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{\mathrm{i}} & \hat{\mathrm{j}} & \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ 4 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & -3 & 6\end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}=6 \hat{i}-20 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}d=\frac{\sqrt{580}}{7}{/tex}

OR

Find the coordinates of the foot of the perpendicular Q drawn from  P(3, 2, 1) to the plane 2x – y + z + 1 = 0. Also, find the distance PQ and the image of the point P treating this plane as a mirror.

Solution:

Here we have given point: P(3, 2, 1) and equation of the plane, 2x – y + z + 1 = 0
Required equation of line is
{tex}\vec{r}=2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}+\mu(2 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}+6 \hat{k}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{1}=-2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}_{2}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}, {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=4 \hat{i}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
d = {tex}\frac{\left|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}\right|}{|\vec{b}|}{/tex}
{tex}\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \times \vec{b}{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 4 & 0 & 2 \\ 2 & -3 & 6 \end{array}\right|{/tex}
{tex}6 \hat{i}-20 \hat{j}-12 \hat{k}{/tex}
d = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{580}}{7}{/tex}

Also, let the foot of the ladder be x m away from the wall. Then, by Pythagoras theorem, we have: {tex} x^2+y^2=169 {/tex} .. [length of the ladder is 13 m] {tex} \Rightarrow y=\sqrt{169-x^2} {/tex} Then, the rate of change of height {tex}(y){/tex} with respect to time {tex}(t){/tex} is given by, {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{-{x}}{\sqrt{169-{x}^2}} \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} Now, when {tex}x=5 {~m}{/tex} , we have: {tex} \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}=\frac{-2 \times 5}{\sqrt{169-5^2}}=\frac{-10}{\sqrt{144}}=-\frac{5}{6}{/tex} Hence, the height of the ladder on the wall is decreasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{5}{6} {~cm} / {sec}{/tex} . 4 23. A dietician wishes to mix two types of food in such a way that the vitamin contents of the mixture contains at least 8 units of vitamin A and 10 units of vitamin C. Food I contains 2 units/kg of vitamin A and 1 unit/kg of vitamin C. It costs ₹ 50 per kg to produce food I. Food II contains 1 unit/kg of vitamin A and 2 units/kg of vitamin C and it costs ₹ 70 per kg to produce food II. Formulate this problem as a LPP to minimise the cost of a mixture that will produce the required diet. Also find the minimum cost. Solution: Let the mixture contain x kg of Food I and y kg of Food II. Clearly, {tex}x \geq 0{/tex} , {tex}y \geq 0{/tex} . We make the following table from the given data: Resources Food Requirement I (x) II (y) Vitamin A (units/kg) 2 1 8 Vitamin C (units/kg) 1 2 10 Cost (Rs/kg) 50 70 Since the mixture must contain at least 8 units of vitamin A and 10 units of vitamin C, we have the constraints: 2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 8 x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 10 Total cost Z of purchasing x kg of food I and y kg of Food II is Z = 50x + 70y Hence, the mathematical formulation of the problem is: Minimise Z = 50x + 70y ....(i) subject to the constraints: 2x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 8 ... (ii) x + 2y {tex}\ge{/tex} 10 ...(iii) x, y {tex}\ge{/tex} 0 .....(iv) Let us graph the inequalities (ii) to (iv). The feasible region determined by the system is shown in Figure. Here again, observe that the feasible region is unbounded. Let us evaluate Z at the corner points A(0,8), B(2,4) and C(10,0). Corner point Z = 50x + 70y (0, 8) 560 (2, 4) 380 Minimum (10, 0) 500 In the table, we find that smallest value of Z is 380 at the point (2,4). Can we say that the minimum value of Z is 380 . Remember that the feasible region is unbounded. Therefore, we have to draw the graph of the inequality 50x + 70y < 380 i.e., 5x + 7y < 38 , to check whether the resulting open half plane has any point common with the feasible region. From the Figure, we see that it has no points in common. Thus, the minimum value of Z is 380 attained at the point (2, 4). Hence, the optimal mixing strategy for the dietician would be to mix 2 kg of Food ‘I’ and 4 kg of Food ‘II’, and with this strategy, the minimum cost of the mixture will be Rs 380. 6 29.
